title: Angry TSMC employees considering strikes, unionization over employee bonuses, report claims
gist: A DigiTimes report says TSMC employees are discussing forming a union and striking after rumors of a ~15% bonus cut despite record Q1 profits, with workers citing Samsung's and SK Hynix's recent union profit-sharing deals as templates; analysts attribute the squeeze to TSMC's $52-56B annual capex building 12 new fabs.

insights.claim: TSMC employees are openly discussing forming a union and staging a strike after rumors the company plans to cut performance bonuses by approximately 15%, per a DigiTimes report.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: First serious labor unrest at union-free TSMC introduces a new operational/cost risk factor; monitor for escalation ahead of the shareholder meeting.
insights.quote: TSMC employees are openly discussing forming a union and staging a strike after rumors spread that the company plans to cut performance bonuses by approximately 15%

insights.claim: The rumored bonus cut comes despite TSMC posting a record Q1 net profit of NT$572.5 billion ($17.9 billion), a 58% year-over-year increase driven by surging AI chip demand.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Record profits alongside a bonus cut sharpens the labor grievance and the profit-allocation debate; a reputational and retention risk for the leading foundry.
insights.quote: posting a record first-quarter net profit of NT$572.5 billion ($17.9 billion), a 58% year-over-year increase

insights.claim: Workers say TSMC's historical practice of returning roughly 13% of retained earnings as employee bonuses has been cut even as profits climb, with the 2025 average employee bonus around NT$2.64 million (~$87,000) and a total pool near NT$206.1 billion.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Quantifies the profit-sharing baseline workers are defending; a shift in this ratio directly affects retention economics at the world's most strategic chipmaker.
insights.quote: the average TSMC employee bonus was approximately NT$2.64 million (roughly $87,000), with the total bonus pool reaching around NT$206.1 billion

insights.claim: TSMC responded that it expects employee profit-sharing bonuses to grow faster in 2026 than in 2025 and that it is fully aware of its growing corporate social responsibility in Taiwan.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Company pushback complicates the rumor; watch actual 2026 bonus disclosures to judge whether unrest is warranted or overstated.
insights.quote: it expects employee profit-sharing bonuses to grow faster in 2026 than in 2025

insights.claim: Analysts attribute the rumored bonus cut to TSMC's capex program: the company is spending $52-56 billion annually while constructing 12 new fabs across the US, Japan, Germany, and Taiwan to secure its lead in 2nm and 1.4nm manufacturing.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Frames a direct capex-vs-compensation tradeoff; the scale of simultaneous fab construction is tightening free cash and creating internal tension worth tracking.
insights.quote: The company is spending $52 billion to $56 billion annually while constructing 12 new fabs across the U.S., Japan, Germany, and Taiwan

insights.claim: Samsung narrowly avoided an 18-day factory shutdown by agreeing to allocate 10.5% of its semiconductor division's operating profit as stock-based bonuses plus 1.5% in cash over 10 years, translating to projected average payouts of roughly $340,000 per chip-division employee in 2026.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Samsung's ~$340K projected payout vs TSMC's ~$87K creates a stark peer comparison fueling TSMC unrest—a benchmark that could reset regional chip-labor cost expectations.
insights.quote: projected average payouts of roughly $340,000 per chip division employee in 2026
insights.locator: The unrest at TSMC has intensified

insights.claim: SK Hynix agreed last September to set aside 10% of operating profit for employee bonuses, while TSMC has operated without any labor union since its founding in 1987 and workers have no formal collective bargaining mechanism.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Korean peers institutionalizing fixed profit-sharing pressures union-free TSMC; a potential structural shift in Taiwan chip labor relations to monitor.
insights.quote: the company has operated without a labor union since its founding in 1987
insights.locator: SK hynix agreed to a similar structure

insights.claim: Samsung's union deal is already facing a shareholder lawsuit arguing that committing a fixed percentage of operating profit to employee payouts over a decade conflicts with the capital demands of chipmaking at scale.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Highlights the core tension between fixed labor profit-sharing and capital-intensive fab expansion—a governance/capital-allocation risk relevant across all three memory/foundry majors.
insights.quote: committing a fixed percentage of operating profit to employee payouts over a decade conflicts with the capital demands of chipmaking at scale
insights.locator: Samsung's deal, meanwhile, is already facing legal pushback.

insights.claim: GlobalWafers chairperson Doris Hsu argued that across the company's 18 factories in nine countries, the key factor in business performance is whether a company shares profits with workers, not whether a union exists.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: An industry-leader framing that reframes the debate from unionization to profit-sharing—useful lens for assessing whether TSMC can defuse unrest without a union.
insights.quote: the key factor in business performance is whether a company shares profits with workers, not whether a union exists
insights.locator: Doris Hsu, chairperson of silicon wafer manufacturer GlobalWafers
